coredata.dc:title: Multiple-color room-temperature phosphorescence regulated by graphitization and carbonyls
coredata.dc:creator: Gan, Lanlan
Zhu, Ying
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Room-temperature phosphorescence with multiple-color activated the widespread interest owing to their promising applications, howbeit, there still exist the insurmountable challenge, especially for elucidating their photoluminescence mechanism and tunable emissions. Here we systematically proposed several carbon dots (CDs) with different degrees of graphitization and amounts of carbonyl while sulfuric acid and ethanol played the critical roles. In brief, sulfuric acid functioned as the carbonization and strong oxidization reagent, while the increasing volumes of ethanol gradually reduced the boiling point of the reactions. Significantly, the energy of these phosphorescent CDs transferred from their excited triplets to oxygen molecules once they were irradiated by UV in aqueous, resulting in the production of the singlet oxygen to oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine. This finding indirectly provided the evidence for the existence of the phosphorescence rather than delayed fluorescence. Towards the photoluminescence mechanism, their fluorescence of CDs was originated from π-π* transition of CC in the carbon cores and n-π* transition of CO on the surfaces, while RTP was mainly attributed to n-π* transition of the carbonyls. The further evidence suggested that the CDs with different graphitization degrees and amounts of CO regulated their multiple-color phosphorescence. Besides, we employed these CDs for information encryption and preparing LED lamps.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room-temperature phosphorescence
Multi-color
Sulfuric acid
Graphitization degree
Amounts of carbonyl

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the multi-color phosphorescent CDs
Preparation of the multiple-color phosphorescence CDs
Elucidating the mechanism of regulating their FL and RTP of these CDs
The critical role of H2SO4 and ethanol for the multi-color phosphorescence
Application of these phosphorescent CDs as the optical ink

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Preparation of the multiple luminescence CDs through adjusting the volume ratio of water to ethanol; (B) Images of these CDs with sunlight, UV on or UV off.

Afterglow intensity of CDs-1 (A), CDs-3 (B), CDs-6 (C) with varied temrperature; (D) Comparing the oxidation performance of TMB by CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6, Inset: image of TMB oxidized by CDs-1, CDs-3, CDs-6; (E) Detection of singlet oxygen produced by CDs-6 through SOSG; (F) Oxidation of TMB by varied concentrations of CDs-6; (G) Functions of oxygen and UV during CDs-6 oxidizing TMB; (H) Illustration of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 producing singlet oxygen and oxidizing TMB.
TEM images of CDs-1 (A), CDs-3 (B) and CDs-6 (C); (D) FTIR spectra of three CDs; Fluorescence (E) and phosphorescence (F) lifetime decay of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6.

(A) XRD spectra of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6; (B) Raman spectra of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6; (C) Absorption spectra, fluorescence and phosphorescence excitation spectra of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6; (D) Relationship between the energy band gaps and the fluorescence of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6; (E) Illustration of the increased graphitization degree and carbonyls of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6.
Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra at 77 K of CDs-1 (A), CDs-3 (B) and CDs-6 (C); (D) Spectra-Jablonski diagram of illustrating the varied ΔEST of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6.

(A) Fluorescence spectra, (B) phosphorescence spectra and (C) the corresponding images of six CDs by dicyandiamide and H3PO4 with varied ratios of H2O to ethanol; (D) Fluorescence spectra, (E) phosphorescence spectra and (F) the corresponding images of six CDs by dicyandiamide and various volume of H2SO4 without ethanol; (G) Fluorescence spectra, (H) phosphorescence spectra and (I) the corresponding images of six CDs by melamine and H2SO4 with varied ratios of H2O to ethanol.

Images of the patterns painted by CDs-1 (A), CDs-3 (B) and CDs-6 (C) with UV on or off; (D) Dual-channel encrypted information (2020, 2021) by CDs-1 and CDs-6; (E) Designed code of LUCKY encrypted by CDs-1; (F) Photoluminescence spectra of the LED prepared here, Inset: LED with sunlight (a) or in the dark (b); (G) Corresponding CIE coordinates of CDs-6-based LED.
Mechanism for the graphitization degree and amount of CO regulating the FL and RTP emissions of CDs.

head.ce:title: Multiple-color room-temperature phosphorescence regulated by graphitization and carbonyls

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room-temperature phosphorescence with multiple-color activated the widespread interest owing to their promising applications, howbeit, there still exist the insurmountable challenge, especially for elucidating their photoluminescence mechanism and tunable emissions. Here we systematically proposed several carbon dots (CDs) with different degrees of graphitization and amounts of carbonyl while sulfuric acid and ethanol played the critical roles. In brief, sulfuric acid functioned as the carbonization and strong oxidization reagent, while the increasing volumes of ethanol gradually reduced the boiling point of the reactions. Significantly, the energy of these phosphorescent CDs transferred from their excited triplets to oxygen molecules once they were irradiated by UV in aqueous, resulting in the production of the singlet oxygen to oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine. This finding indirectly provided the evidence for the existence of the phosphorescence rather than delayed fluorescence. Towards the photoluminescence mechanism, their fluorescence of CDs was originated from π-π* transition of CC in the carbon cores and n-π* transition of CO on the surfaces, while RTP was mainly attributed to n-π* transition of the carbonyls. The further evidence suggested that the CDs with different graphitization degrees and amounts of CO regulated their multiple-color phosphorescence. Besides, we employed these CDs for information encryption and preparing LED lamps.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials, emerging as the prospective functional materials, have attracted the tremendous attention due to their potential applications in the fields of biochemical imaging [1], optical detecting [2], intelligence security [3,4] and beyond. Generally, the efficient intersystem crossing (ISC) and the corresponding triplet excitons play the critical role towards promoting the occurrence of RTP [5]. Nevertheless, the conventional RTP materials basically consist of the rare earth element, noble metal complex or pure organics, and the long-term application are therewith restricted by their biological toxicity [6], exorbitant price [7], relatively weak spin–orbit coupling of electrons [8]. Consequently, it is of great value to develop the promising non-metal RTP candidates to meet the requirements of multiple phosphorescent-material industries, especially for the aspects of information security and light emitting diode (LED) lamps [9,10].

Carbon dots (CDs), existing as one class of carbon-based luminescent material, show a wide range of applications including bioimaging [11], biochemical sensing [12], catalysis [13–15], light-emitting diodes [16], drug delivery [17], photothermal therapy [18] owing to their advantage of the facile preparations, satisfactory photostability, low toxicity and tunable emissions [19–22]. Basically, the majority of the current exploration towards CDs focused on their fluorescent property [23], luminescent mechanism [24] and related applications [25]. Currently, the effective constructions of CDs-based RTP materials mainly include the matrix-assisted system [26] and self-protective structure [27]. The intermolecular vibrations of CDs could be suppressed through being embed in the rigid matrix, thus building up the matrix-assisted system and emitting RTP [28]. By contrast, the self-protective structure of CDs is originated from the precursors of the polymer or crosslinking reagents, which enable their surfaces to be equipped with the polymer chains or polymer-like structures, hence protecting them from the external interference [29]. By virtue of the previous ways, these CDs form hydrogen bonds [30], covalent bonds [7] or confined structures [31], thus stabilizing their triplet excited states and further achieving the RTP. However, the phosphorescence mechanism of CDs that underlie the room temperature phosphorescence emission is essential but unknown, and scarce studies were contributed. Meanwhile, the existing phosphorescent CDs generally emit the single-color RTP, since the preparation of multi-color RTP of CDs is still challenging.

The further studies have revealed that the fluorescence origin of CDs mainly derives from the intrinsic and extrinsic emissions, and the intrinsic emission refers to sp2 carbon domain of the cores, while the extrinsic one is regulated by the functional groups on the surfaces. Through changing the synthesis conditions, CDs achieve the more effective conjugation in carbon cores and are equipped with diverse functional groups on their surfaces, thus obtaining the multiple-color fluorescence of CDs [32,33]. For instance, the fluorescent CDs with a wide-wavelength were synthesized by tuning the reaction solvents [34]. Also, the blue and red emission of CDs were prepared by changing the reaction temperature, demonstrating that both carbonyl group (CO) and graphite N play an important role towards the fluorescence (FL) shifting of CDs [35]. Again, synthesizing a series of full-color emission CDs by adjusting the molar ratios between the reaction precursors were accomplished [36].

Significantly, we achieved the successful synthesis of multi-color phosphorescent CDs based on the previous experience of preparing the diverse fluorescence of CDs. In brief, we first employed H2SO4, as well as adjusting the volume ratio of ethanol, and succeeded in preparing the CDs with different graphitization degrees and varying amounts of CO, while dicyandiamine (DCD) also served as a precursor (Scheme 1 ). To be specific, the boiling point of the reaction solvent gradually decreased with increasing the proportion of ethanol during the reactions, leading to the degree of carbonization and dehydration increased. By virtue of the dehydration and oxidization activity H2SO4, the graphitization degree and particle size of the CDs also increased, and the strong oxidization of H2SO4 resulted in an increased amount of CO. The further experiments also provided the detailed evidence for that H2SO4 and ethanol were involved in the degree of graphitization and the amount of CO. Our deeper exploration elucidated that both FL and RTP emission of these CDs were regulated by the graphitization degree and amount of CO (Scheme 1). Our work was the first to explore the functions of the graphitization degree and amount of CO on RTP, which provided the solid support for revealing the phosphorescence mechanism of CDs. Besides, the proposed multi-color and dual-channel emissions of CDs here showed the potential in the fields of advanced information decryption and preparing light emitting diode (LED) lamps.

Dicyandiamide (DCD) and Melamine were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China), while the solvents of ethanol and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) as well as phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were acquired from Chron Chemicals Co., ltd (Chengdu, China). Also, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., ltd (Shanghai, China) as well as sulfuric acid (H2SO4) from Chongqing chuandong chemical Co., ltd (Chongqing, China). Again, the Singlet Oxygen Sensor Green Reagent was acquired from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., ltd. The microwave oven of Galanz (Shunde, China) was utilized towards the syntheses. Meanwhile, these commercial reagents were directly applied without any purification, and we obtained the ultrapure water through an Aquapro AWL-0502-P water system of 18.25 MΩ·cm.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of high-resolution JEOL JEM 2100F was employed to obtain the morphology images of the proposed CDs. All the fluorescent and phosphorescent analyses were achieved by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), while the UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by using a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). And the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra were recorded through a UV-3600Plus (Tokyo, Japan). Similarly, the Thermo escalab 250xi X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer were introduced to analyze the chemical compositions and the functional groups. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired to identify the crystallinity based on a Brucker D8 Advance (Karlsruhe, Germany), and the Raman spectra were performed using a Raman Spectrometer of Renishaw inVia (Gloucestershire,UK). Besides, all the lifetime was measured with a HORIBA FL-3 spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan) at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the multi-color phosphorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The various CDs described here were synthesized on the basis of the microwave-assisted method. Typically, the paralleled dicyandiamide of 1.0 g were respectively transferred to a crucible followed by adding different solutions with varied volume ratios (4:0, 3:1, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3 for H2O to ethanol), and then the equal sulfuric acids (each for 500 µL) were separately introduced followed by the gentle stirring. Finally, these mixtures were subjected to the microwave oven and reacted for 90 s with the medium–high fire to obtain the rude product of CDs.

Initially, 0.12 g of TMB was dissolved with 50.0 mL of DMSO followed by the cold storage away from light, and 0.05 g powder of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 were similarly dissolved in 10 mL of DMSO, respectively. To be specific, 150 μL of previously prepared CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 were added into three eppendorf (EP) tubes of 1.5 mL, and 150 μL of parallel TMB solutions were introduced to the EP tubes above followed by diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water. After half an hour of UV irradiation in the presence of oxygen, the corresponding UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded. To identify the optimal amount of CDs-6 for oxidizing TMB, various concentrations of CDs-6 (0.25 mg•mL−1, 0.50 mg•mL−1, 0.75 mg•mL−1, 1.00 mg•mL−1 and 1.25 mg•mL−1, each for 150 μL) and 150 μL of TMB were successively added to each EP tube followed by diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water, and the UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained. Towards exploring the functions of oxygen and UV for activating the singlet oxygen, 300 μL of mixtures (CDs-6 and TMB) were parallelly introduced into three EP tubes with or without oxygen, without UV for 30 min, and their UV–visible absorption spectra were thereupon measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the multiple-color phosphorescence CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, we first employed H2SO4 to prepare the CDs with the multiple-color afterglow (named as CDs-1, CDs-2, CDs-3, CDs-4, CDs-5, CDs-6, respectively), while the mixture of H2O and ethanol with various volume ratios served as the reaction solvents (Fig. 1 A). Meanwhile, the detailed preparation procedures were described in Experimental Section (Figure S1, Supporting Information). The obtained powder of these CDs showed the fluorescence from blue to yellow with UV on, and the corresponding afterglow ranging from green to orange by the naked eyes. Upon the excitation by 365 nm of UV, their FL and RTP emissions of the powder for six CDs exhibited the gradual red-shifting with the volume of ethanol increasing (Fig. 1B).

To address whether the afterglow of these CDs were phosphorescence or delayed fluorescence, we initially investigated their afterglow variations with temperature changing. Thus, we selected CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 as the typical representatives, which basically covered the relatively comparative color range. With the temperature increasing, the afterglow intensity of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 gradually decreased under the excitation of 365 nm (Fig. 2 A, 2B and 2C), providing the evidence that the emitted afterglow of these CDs were the phosphorescent emissions rather than thermally activated delayed fluorescence [37].

Since the energy of the triple-states excitons could transfer to oxygen molecules, thus producing the singlet oxygen (1O2), which could oxidize TMB to be oxTMB with the visible-blue emerging. To further confirm that the afterglow of these CDs were phosphorescence, we investigated whether TMB could be oxidized as blue in the absence of oxygen and UV. Consequently, we explored the RTP generation pathway through detecting the photooxidation activity of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 using the way previously reported, because RTP could promote the production of 1O2 to oxidize TMB [38], and a characteristic UV–vis absorption peak appears at 652 nm once TMB is oxidized. In particular, CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 all showed the obvious function of oxidizing TMB with 365 nm of UV, and the results described that CDs-6 exhibited the highest oxidation effect than CDs-1 and CDs-3, and the related color difference were observed in daylight (Fig. 2D). To test whether these CDs could activate oxygen to produce 1O2, CDs-6 was selected. Singlet Oxygen Sensor Green Reagent (SOSG) was thereupon utilized to detect 1O2, since it shows the highly selective response to 1O2 rather than hydroxyl radical (∙OH) or superoxide radical anion (∙O2 –), and the related emission of 525 nm excited at 504 nm could be observed once 1O2 exists. To be specific, the fluorescence peak of SOSG at 525 nm appeared in the presence of CDs-6, indicating that CDs-6 produced 1O2 (Fig. 2E). Again, we chose CDs-6 to perform the deeper study, and the absorption intensity of oxTMB gradually increased accompanied by the concentration of CDs-6 rising (Fig. 2F), indicating that the amount of CDs-6 was involved in the generation efficiency of 1O2. The corresponding image for the color of oxTMB gradually changing darker with the increased CDs-6 was also recorded (Fig. 2F). Furthermore, the oxidation of TMB by CDs-6 in the absence of oxygen or UV was obviously inhibited (Fig. 2G), demonstrating that both the production of 1O2 and UV radiation were required for CDs-6 oxidizing TMB. These findings indirectly confirmed that afterglow of these current CDs were phosphorescence, since the excited triplet of phosphorescence, but not the delayed fluorescence, could transfer O2 to be 1O2 and further achieve the oxidation of TMB. Additionally, these CDs also showed the potentiality of efficiently oxidizing TMB for the colorimetric analysis.

Taken together, the energy of the lowest excited triplet state (T1) for these phosphorescent CDs transferred to O2 and produced 1O2, and the generation efficiency of 1O2 played a critical role during the process of oxidizing TMB, and improving ISC between S1 (the lowest excited singlet state) and T1 could facilitate the production of 1O2 (Fig. 2H). Among these phosphorescent CDs, CDs-6 with the longest emission showed the strongest activity of producing 1O2, and thus exhibiting the highest oxidation of TMB (Fig. 2H).

To systematically explore the phosphorescent CDs prepared here, their fluorescent and phosphorescent performance was initially recorded. To be specific, the powder of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 respectively showed their optimal fluorescence emission at 398 nm, 485 nm and 550 nm (Fig. S2A-S2C) as well as their corresponding maximum RTP emissions at 500 nm, 529 nm, 565 nm (Fig. S2E-S2G). Simultaneously, the FL and RTP of the three CDs depended on their excitations (Figure S3), indicating the existence of different luminescent centers [39]. Also, the Commission International de l’ Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of three CDs were calculated through their FL and RTP spectra excited at 365 nm as shown in Table S1, Supporting Information, describing their emission locations and the color variation of fluorescence and RTP (Fig. S2D and H, Supporting Information). Again, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of CDs-1, CDs-3, and CDs-6 with 365 nm of excitation were 36.2 %, 15.1 % and 4.23 %, and the absolute phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 were similarly obtained as 14.5 %, 10.7 % and 3.3 %, describing that both FLQY and PQY of three CDs were negatively related with their emission wavelengths. These finding demonstrated that their FL and RTP of the three CDs showed a tendency of red shifting with the excitation of 365 nm.

The morphology of three phosphorescent CDs were subsequently investigated by high resolution transmission electron microscopy, describing that these CDs dispersed well and exhibited as the spherical particles with the average diameters of 13.54 nm (Fig. 3 A), 18.84 nm (Fig. 3B) and 20.15 nm (Fig. 3C), respectively. To identify their elemental compositions and functional groups, three kinds of CDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Basically, four similar and characteristic peaks of their full XPS spectra appeared at 169.03, 284.79, 399.94, and 532.13 eV for three CDs, proving that they mainly consisted of C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p (Figure S4). In particular, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s for CDs-1 was resolved as three peaks at 288.98, 286.00 and 284.89 eV, which were attributed to the groups of CC/CC, CO/CN and CO/CN (Figure S5A. The N1s spectrum (Figure S5B) was converted into pyridinic N (399.20 eV), pyrollic N (400.25 eV) and graphitic N (401.80 eV), while its O1s spectra consisted of two components, corresponding to CO at 532.02 eV and CO at 533.00 eV (Figure S5C). Again, two components of S 2p at 168.85 eV and 169.90 eV were fitted, referring to the sulphone bridges of -C-SOx-C- (Figure S5D) [40]. Meanwhile, the high-resolution XPS spectra demonstrated that CDs-3 (Figure S5E-5H) and CDs-6 (Figure S5I-5L) were equipped with the same groups as CDs-1. These findings by XPS revealed that there existed the functional groups of carbonyl, hydroxyl and amino on their surfaces of CDs-1. Importantly, the contents of CC/CC for CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 were separately calculated to be 65.97 %, 67.97 % and 73.68 % as well as that of CO for 26.27 %, 37.04 % and 72.78 %, respectively (Table S2), demonstrating that three CDs with the multi-color phosphorescence were associated with the increased graphitization degree and carbonyls. To further confirm the functional groups of three CDs, Fourier infrared spectroscopy was employed (Fig. 3D). A broad absorption band in the range 3100–3500 cm−1 described the stretching vibrations of OH/NH, and the characteristic peaks located at 1659.26 and 1472.96 cm−1 were separately assigned to the stretching vibration of CO and bending vibration of CN. The peak at 1240.23 and 1043.49 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of SO and bending vibration of CO. These findings showed a general agreement with that of XPS.

To obtain their fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetime, the time-resolved photoluminescence measurements of three CDs were performed (Fig. 3E). Since the time- resolved decay curve of CDs could be fitted by a multiple-exponent formula, the average luminescence lifetimes were accordingly calculated using the following formula:
(1)
τ
average
= ∑
α i
τ
i
2
/ ∑
α i
τ i

Here, τ1 and τ2 represent the fitted lifetimes as well as
α
1 and
α
2 for the corresponding proportions of τ1 and τ2. In brief, the FL lifetime of CDs-1, CDs-3, and CDs-6 were calculated as 9.96, 6.34 and 5.60 ns (Fig. 3E). According to the phosphorescence decays of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 (Fig. 3F), their related lifetime were calculated to be 189.24, 92.85, and 85.09 ms. Consistent with the previously reported, the property of excitation dependence and bi-exponential decay (Table S3 and S4) revealed the existence of multiple emission centers for these prepared CDs [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elucidating the mechanism of regulating their FL and RTP of these CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To deeply understand their multiple-color luminescence mechanism, X-ray diffraction patterns of the three CDs prepared here were obtained. There exhibited a broad peak centered at around 26.86° for three CDs (Fig. 4
A), and the interlayer distance was calculated as 0.33 nm through the formula of 2dsinθ = nλ, showing similar as that of graphite (0.34 nm). This result indicated that their cores of CDs were dominated by the high-conjugated sp2 domain [32], and the slight and progressive increase of the peak intensity for CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 might describe their increased graphitization degree. Meanwhile, the Raman spectra of three CDs showed two peaks located at 1345 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1 (Fig. 4B), and D band corresponded to the disordered structure as well as G band for the graphitic carbon domain, and the ratio of ID/IG directly indicates the graphitization degree. Accordingly, the ratios of ID/IG for CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 were calculated to be 1.16, 1.01 and 0.91, suggesting that their graphitization degree gradually increased from CDs-1 to CDs-6, which further confirmed the result of XRD. Theoretically, the increase of graphitization degree demonstrates the enlarged sp2 conjugated domain of carbon cores. This finding was consistent with the previous study in which the carbon cores of CDs are partially responsible for their FL emission wavelengths [42].

There existed the obvious peaks at 286 nm for all the UV–vis spectra of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6, which were closely related to the π-π* transition of CC in the carbon cores (Fig. 4C), and these tailing peaks simultaneously extended to 300–500 nm as the result of n-π* transition of CO on the surfaces [43]. Also, the intensity of these tailing peaks gradually increased from CDs-1 to CDs-6, demonstrating the increased amounts of CO, which was consistent with our previous findings. Furthermore, the FL excitation spectra of the three CDs covered two bands of the UV–vis absorption spectra, indicating that their fluorescence were derived from the π-π* transition of CC and n-π* transition of CO. Through combining the RTP excitation and absorption spectra of three CDs, all their maximal excitation highly overlapped with the related tailing peaks of the absorption, proving that their RTP were mainly originated from the n-π* transition of CO. All these findings suggested that the amounts of CO on their surfaces played an important role for the FL and RTP emissions.

As been widely recognized, the emissions of photoluminescent materials are closely associated with the energy band gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [44]. It has been also proved that the larger surface-oxidation degree and sp2 conjugated domain collectively lead to the smaller energy band gap of CDs, revealing that there exist the more surface groups of CO and the increased graphite degree [36]. To further clarify the cause for multiple-color FL of these CDs, we thereupon explored the relationship between the energy band gap and CO. Particularly, as shown in Fig. 4D, the band gap of CDs-1 was calculated to be 3.37 eV (Fig. S6A and S6D). Likewise, the band gap for CDs-3 was obtained to be 2.79 eV (Fig. S6B and S6E) as well as 2.26 eV for CDs-6 (Fig. S6C and S6F). Taken together, the energy band gaps of CDs-1 to CDs-6 gradually decreased, corresponding to the increased carbonyls and graphite degree (Fig. 4E), which showed an agreement with the previous report. Accordingly, we proposed that the amount of CO and graphite degree cooperatively regulated their FL emission wavelengths of CDs.

We preliminarily proved that the n-π* transition of CO served as the origin of the RTP emissions of these CDs. To deeply understand the role of CO during their RTP emissions of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6, we further performed the low-temperature (77 K) FL and RTP spectra with the excitation of 365 nm. The singlet–triplet energy splitting (ΔEST) between the lowest excited singlet (S1) and the lowest excited triplet (T1) were quantitatively evaluated, since the vibration dissipation achieves being minimized at the low-temperature of 77 K. Particularly, the FL emissions of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 were respectively recorded as 413 nm, 450 nm and 510 nm with the excitation of 365 nm (Fig. 5A, 5B and 5C), and thus their energy levels of S1 were obtained as 3.00 eV, 2.75 eV and 2.43 eV, while those of T1 were 2.47 eV, 2.39 eV and 2.23 eV. Based on the previous calculations, we subsequently established a spectra-Jablonski diagram (Fig. 5D). Briefly, the excited electrons by 365 nm of UV for CDs-1 immediately relax to the lowest excited singlet state (S1‘) through internal conversion (IC), and these electrons return to the S0 state, thus emitting the blue fluorescence (S1‘→S0). Alternatively, the S1‘ electrons transfer to the lowest excited triplet state (T1‘) via ISC and return to the S0 state, and resulting in the emergence of the green RTP (S1‘⋯ T1‘→S0) [45]. Again, the FL and RTP emissions of CDs-3 and CDs-6 were provided by the similar way (Fig. 5D). Accordingly, their ΔEST of CDs-1, CDs-3, CDs-6 were calculated to be 0.53 eV, 0.36 eV and 0.20 eV, respectively (Fig. 5D). As been well known, increasing CO could enhance the spin–orbit coupling, thus reducing ΔEST between S1 and T1 to promote the occurrence of efficient ISC and produce the more triplet excitons. Taken together, the gradually decreased ΔEST from CDs-1 to CDs-6 agreed with the increased amount of CO on their surfaces. Compared with CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 preferred to emit the corresponding phosphorescence, since the ΔEST of CDs-3 and CDs-6 were lower than that of CDs-1. Thus, the multi-color RTP were acquired here.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The critical role of H2SO4 and ethanol for the multi-color phosphorescence
ce:section.ce:para: To identify the roles of H2SO4 and ethanol towards the multi-color phosphorescence of these CDs, we performed the further experiments. When we only replaced H2SO4 by H3PO4 with the other identical synthesis conditions, the six CDs emitted the same blue fluorescence and green phosphorescence with the varied proportions of H2O to ethanol (Fig. 6A, 6B and 6C). Compared with H3PO4, H2SO4 showed the stronger activity of dehydrating and oxidizing, thus we could not obtain the multi-color phosphorescent CDs with H3PO4. Subsequently, we employed H2O without ethanol as the reaction solvent to prepare a series of phosphorescent CDs. Along the volume of H2SO4 varying from 500 μL to 700 μL, these CDs showed the single-color FL and RTP as that synthesized by H3PO4 (Fig. 6D, 6E and 6F). Because the boiling point of the reaction was relatively high in the absence of ethanol, leading to the insufficient dehydration and oxidation of H2SO4. To address whether the multi-color phosphorescence of the CDs depended on H2SO4 and ethanol, we introduced melamine as the precursor to replace DCD in the presence of H2SO4 and ethanol, and the multi-color FL and RTP of CDs emerged again (Fig. 6G, 6H and 6I). Taken together, these findings demonstrated that both H2SO4 and ethanol were critical for synthesizing the multi-color phosphorescent CDs.

More specifically, the addition of concentrated H2SO4 not only achieved the rapid dehydration and graphitization of the precursor, but also increased the oxidation degree of their surfaces. Meanwhile, the presence of ethanol reduced the boiling point of the reaction solvent, and the boiling point gradually decreased with increasing the proportion of ethanol during the reaction process. Consequently, both H2SO4 and ethanol were indispensable and played the important role for preparing the CDs with the multi-color phosphorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of these phosphorescent CDs as the optical ink
ce:section.ce:para:
Taking advantage of their multi-color FL and RTP, we thereupon utilized these CDs as the optical ink for dual-channel painting and information encryption. Specifically, the powder of CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6 were applied to paint three patterns on the paper, and these graphics respectively showed the blue, green and yellow fluorescence with UV on, while the green, yellow and orange phosphorescence appeared with UV off (Fig. 7
A, 7B and 7C). In addition, these CDs were employed for information encryption. Briefly, the real information of 2020 and 2021 were hidden as the design code of 8888 and 8888. The orange domain of the design code were coated with CDs-6, while the blue domain were coated with CDs-3 as well as the white with wet CDs-6. There exhibited the covered message of 8888 and 8888 with UV on, and the decryption message of 2020 and 2021 only appeared with UV off (Fig. 7D). For 10 groups of vertical spots, two groups of vertical spots represented one letter, and the green dots were coated with CDs-1 as well as the white one with wet CDs-1. Accordingly, the correct message was decoded as LUCKY by using the phosphorescent mode (Fig. 7E). More significantly, the CDs-6-based LED was innovatively developed though coating CDs-6 on the commercial UV chip (450 nm) using AB glue. As shown in Fig. 7F and 7G, the maximum emission of CDs-6-based LEDs was 577 nm, and the CIE 1931 coordinates of this LED located at (0.3731, 0.3741), which highly approached the coordinates (0.33, 0.33) of the pure white light. Meanwhile, the correlated color temperature and the color rendering index of the current LED was 4155 K and 79.3, which were similar as that of the warm-white light, suggesting that the CDs-6-based warm-white LED showed the great promise of preparing optoelectronic devices.

We successfully raised a series of CDs with the multiple fluorescence and phosphorescence, while H2SO4 first served as the necessary acid and ethanol was innovatively employed. Initially, their RTP rather than delayed fluorescence were confirmed by these CDs oxidizing TMB through producing the singlet oxygen. Subsequently, CDs-1, CDs-3 and CDs-6, as the represents, were selected to explore the detailed photoluminescence mechanism. We proved that the π-π* transition of CC and n-π* transition of CO were the origin of their fluorescence for these CDs, while their RTP were mainly derived from the n-π* transition of CO. More precisely, the n-π* transitions of CO improved the spin–orbit coupling and promoted the occurrence of ISC, and resulting in RTP emission. Additionally, the increased carbonyls of these CDs lead to the smaller ΔEST, also contributing to their RTP emissions. Meanwhile, both sulfuric acid and ethanol played the essential role during the syntheses, since H2SO4 exhibited the stronger function of dehydration and oxidization, and ethanol provided the lower boiling point. This study facilitated to deeply understand the mechanism of the multi-color RTP. Besides, these CDs were applied in the fields of the dual-channel information encryption and developing LED lamps.

Lanlan Gan: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Ying Zhu: Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoming Yang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0027) and Talent and Innovation Project of Beibei District in Chongqing (2022-11).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.141635.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultralong and efficient phosphorescence from silica confined carbon nanodots in aqueous solution
